perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that the Application Software and all documentation and related materials are proprietary to Company and are confidential and constitute a valuable asset of Company, and that the data files contained in the Confidential Information are proprietary to Client and are confidential and constitute a valuable asset of Client. Recipient agrees to safeguard the Confidential Information, and Recipient shall not disclose or give access to the Confidential Information to any person or entity other than those employees of Recipient who have a need for such access in order to assist Client with Conversion.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that the Application Software and all documentation and related materials are proprietary to Company and are confidential and constitute a valuable asset of Company, and that the data files contained in the Confidential Information are proprietary to Client and are confidential and constitute a valuable asset of Client. Recipient agrees to make commercially reasonable efforts to safeguard the Confidential Information, and Recipient shall not disclose or give access to the Confidential Information to any person or entity.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'safeguard' to 'commercially reasonable efforts to safeguard' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Removing the clause 'other than those employees of Recipient who have a need for such access in order to assist Client with Conversion' further broadens the scope of prohibited disclosures, potentially hindering legitimate business operations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.003 protects trade secrets. Diluting the safeguarding obligation to 'commercially reasonable efforts' might not meet the standard of care required to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets under Florida law. Removing the access exception for employees needing it for conversion purposes could be seen as restricting legitimate use of the data and violating the implied terms related to the agreement's purpose.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires the recipient to 'safeguard the Confidential Information.' The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the recipient only needs to make 'commercially reasonable efforts to safeguard' the information. This creates a contradiction because, as the Law Explanation suggests, this weaker obligation might not meet the standard of care required to maintain trade secret protection under Florida law. Florida Statute 688.003 allows for injunctive relief for 'actual or threatened misappropriation,' implying a need for strong protection of trade secrets, which the original text provided more directly. The modified text could be interpreted as not providing sufficient safeguarding, therefore increasing the risk of misappropriation. Additionally, removing the access exception for employees who need access to assist the Client contradicts implied terms related to the agreement's purpose, potentially hindering legitimate use of the data.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not, and it will not permit anyone else, to copy the Confidential Information or Application Software.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall not copy the Confidential Information or Application Software.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'and it will not permit anyone else' weakens the clause, suggesting Recipient is only responsible for their own actions and not the actions of those they oversee, creating a loophole.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.002(4) defines misappropriation, which includes disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who 'used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret' or 'at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was... acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' By removing the liability of recipient for anyone else that they allow to copy such information weakens the trade secret protection. A company is responsible for not allowing other parties to copy information, not just restraining themselves from copying.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The omission of "and it will not permit anyone else" from the original text directly weakens the recipient's responsibility regarding the confidentiality of the information. Florida Statute 688.002(2)(b) addresses the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' The original text ensures the recipient's obligation extends to preventing others from copying the information, aligning with the statute's intent to protect trade secrets. The modified text potentially allows the recipient to avoid liability if a third party, acting under their watch or with their implicit permission, copies the information. This creates a loophole and a direct contradiction of the broader protective measures intended by trade secret law as mentioned in 688.002(4).

perturbation.original_text: In the event an employee of Recipient terminates his or her employment with Recipient, Recipient agrees to require such terminated employee to immediately return to Recipient all copies of the Confidential Information in such employee's possession at the time of termination of employment.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event an employee of Recipient terminates his or her employment with Recipient, Recipient agrees to request such terminated employee to return all copies of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'require' to 'request' weakens the obligation to retrieve confidential information from departing employees, potentially violating trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.003 requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Changing 'require' to 'request' weakens the measure to retrieve all copies of sensitive information, potentially not meeting reasonable efforts. This could result in a violation of Florida's Trade Secret Act.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'require' to 'request' weakens the obligation to retrieve confidential information. Florida Statute 688.003, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows for court-ordered affirmative acts to protect a trade secret. By changing 'require' to 'request,' the contract moves away from taking reasonable and compulsory steps to protect confidential information, potentially failing to meet the standard of reasonable efforts required to maintain secrecy, which is implied in the statute. This undermines the ability to enforce trade secret protection, as the company is no longer mandating the return of confidential information, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that the use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Application Software by Recipient (including any of its employees or anyone who obtains the Confidential Information or Application Software or gains access thereto from or through Recipient or any of its employees) in a manner inconsistent with this Agreement will cause Company or Client as the case may be, irreparable damage. In such event, Company and Client shall have the right to equitable and injunctive relief to prevent any unauthorized use or disclosure, and to such damages as are occasioned by any such unauthorized use or disclosure, including but not limited to reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred in enforcing Company's or Client's rights hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that the use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Application Software by Recipient in a manner inconsistent with this Agreement may cause Company or Client as the case may be, damage. In such event, Company and Client shall have the right to damages as are occasioned by any such unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'including any of its employees or anyone who obtains the Confidential Information or Application Software or gains access thereto from or through Recipient or any of its employees' limits Recipient's liability for actions of others. Removing 'irreparable' weakens the damage standard. Removing 'equitable and injunctive relief to prevent any unauthorized use or disclosure, and to such damages... including but not limited to reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred in enforcing Company's or Client's rights hereunder' significantly limits the recourse available to Company and Client in case of breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.004 provides for injunctive relief and damages for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the reference to injunctive relief and limiting the damages available, the modified text contradicts the remedies available under Florida's Trade Secret Act, making it harder to enforce trade secret protection.

(2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes key aspects of remedies available under Florida's Trade Secrets Act, as described in the Law Explanation, which is supported by Florida Statute 688.004, cited in the Law Explanation. By removing 'irreparable damage' and the right to 'equitable and injunctive relief,' and limiting recourse to just 'damages,' the changed text significantly weakens the protection offered to trade secrets. This directly contradicts the intent of the Act to provide comprehensive remedies, as indicated by the reference to Chapter 688, which includes sections on 'Injunctive relief' and 'Damages'. Thus, it creates a significant legal contradiction.
